front: City: identified as the home of the mathematician Archimedes and the largest, most powerful Greek city on the island of Sicily
City: founded as a colony of Corinth
City: Byzantine emperor Constans II moved his court here after losing support in Constantinople, and was assassinated here
Ruler: restored the power of the landowning gamaroi elite and defeated the Carthaginian king Hamilcar at the Battle of Himera (480 BCE)
work: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty

front: Ruler: won a key battle partly by sending troops disguised as allies from the city of Selinus
work: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty

front: Ruler: refused to send aid against the Persian invasion of mainland Greece because he was not offered supreme command
work: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty

front: City: credited with inventing the quinquereme warship under the Deinomenid dynasty
work: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty

front: Ruler: praised by the poet Pindar for his chariot-racing victories
work: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty

front: Ruler: defeated the Etruscans and Carthaginians at the Battle of Cumae
work: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty

front: Ruler: last tyrant of the Deinomenid dynasty, who according to Diodorus relied "only upon the mercenaries" before being overthrown in 466 BCE
work: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty

front: Institution: the lower-class killichiroi expelled the ruling gamoroi elite
Institution: during the Expedition of the Ten Thousand, a Syracusan contingent of 300 hoplites was led by Sosis
Institution: a leaf-based voting system used to banish political rivals
Institution: a radical democracy installed at the instigation of Diocles, who exiled the more moderate Hermocrates
Figure: proposed a Pan-Sicilian alliance at the Congress of Gela, later called an "ancient Monroe Doctrine"
Ruler: rose to power amid the chaos following the sack of Akragas
Ruler: conquered nearby Rhegium after enslaving its people
Ruler: fortified the island of Ortygia as his stronghold
Ruler: his war with Carthage saw the destruction of Motya, whose survivors founded Lilybaeum
Ruler: built the stone prison Lautumiae, containing a quarry known as his "Ear," where he tortured prisoners and allegedly imprisoned the poet Philoxenus
Ruler: drank himself to death after his tragedy The Ransom of Hector won first prize at a festival
Ruler: after an argument with Plato over virtue, encouraged the Spartan Pollis to assassinate Plato aboard a galley
Ruler: allegedly sold Plato into slavery
Ruler: forced Arete, wife of an exiled former advisor, to marry Timocrates
Ruler: patronized the court historian Philistus
Ruler: exiled his own uncle Dion, who formed an army at Zacynthus to overthrow him
Ruler: so afraid of assassins that he allegedly only let his daughters cut his hair, using red-hot walnut shells
Ruler: brought an extravagant tent to the Olympic Games, which onlookers trashed
Ruler: gave up the throne to the general Timoleon in exchange for safe passage to Corinth
Event: Syracuse's defense was aided by the Spartan general Gylippus, who defeated both Nicias and Demosthenes
Event: originally meant to be led by Alcibiades, who was recalled to stand trial after the mutilation of statues called herms
Event: led by Nicias, ending in a crushing defeat for Athens during the Peloponnesian War
Event: a barber who tried to spread word of the coming attack was tortured for disturbing the city
Event: defeated Athenian troops trampled each other trying to reach water at the Assinarus River
Event: Nicias was captured at its end
Event: Nicias made the Athenian forces wait 27 days to sail home because of a lunar eclipse, worsening the disaster
Ruler: shunned in his home city of Corinth for helping kill his own brother Timophanes
Ruler: defeated the rival strongman Hicetas of Leontini to secure control of Syracuse
Ruler: won the Battle of the Crimissus over a Carthaginian army under Asdrubal, aided by a driving hailstorm
Ruler: set up a new constitution for Syracuse after his victory at the Crimissus
Ruler: paired with the Roman general Lucius Aemilius Paullus in Plutarch's Parallel Lives
Ruler: overthrew the government Timoleon had installed to become tyrant of Syracuse
Ruler: proclaimed himself king of Sicily
Ruler: hired the Campanian mercenaries known as the Mamertines
Ruler: after a defeat at Himera, invaded the Carthaginian homeland in Africa in 307 BCE, winning two battles at White Tunis
Ruler: his troops were decimated by drinking the salty water of the Himera River before this African invasion
Ruler: concluded a false treaty with the governor Ophellas to lure him into attacking Carthage, then murdered him and absorbed his troops
Ruler: was eventually defeated by a general of Pyrrhus of Epirus who took the Syracusan throne as Hiero II
Ruler: a former general of Pyrrhus of Epirus, who saved Syracuse from a siege before this man took over and renewed conflicts with Carthage
Ruler: joined Rome during the First Punic War, sparked when his troops defeated the Mamertine mercenaries
Ruler: minted coinage bearing the image of his wife Philistis, whom he married to gain aristocratic support after Pyrrhus departed Sicily
Ruler: first appointed strategos by the oligarchic party after making his reputation fighting the Bruttians
Ruler: suspected a goldsmith of stealing gold meant for a votive crown, and had a mathematician test whether it was pure
Ruler: commissioned an enormous grain ship with gardens grown on its decks, possibly the largest ship in the ancient world
Ruler: his death in 215 BCE ended a long era of Sicilian loyalty to Rome
Ruler: succeeded his grandfather as a teenager and ruled less than a year before being assassinated
War: Euryalus Castle was defended from a Carthaginian cavalry assault led by the exiled defector Deinocrates
War: a civil war between Thoenon and Sostratus forced Syracuse to call in the foreign king Pyrrhus of Epirus for aid, whose wife Lanassa had ties to the city
War: fought across generations by rulers such as Dionysius I of Syracuse and the Carthaginian commander Hamilcar the Magonid
Institution: lifted a siege of a Greek colony on the island of Hvar by defeating the Illyrian Liburnians at the Battle of Pharos
Institution: used animal hides to deflect enemy grappling hooks in battle
Institution: was crushed by the Carthaginian commander Mago at the Battle of Catana, leading to an unsuccessful siege of Syracuse
Institution: combined with forces from Cumae, the first Greek colony on the Italian mainland, to defeat the Etruscans in the Bay of Naples
Institution: was victorious at the Battle of Cumae and helped defeat the Athenian expedition led by Nicias
Event: after the teenaged tyrant Hieronymus broke Syracuse's alliance with Rome, the city allied with Carthage under the magistrates Hippocrates and Epicydes
work: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes

front: Event: besieged by the Roman consul Marcus Claudius Marcellus beginning in 214 BCE during the Second Punic War
work: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes

front: Event: a festival for Artemis allowed Marcellus's forces to infiltrate the city, aided by the traitor Moeriscus
work: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes

front: Event: Moeriscus's betrayal enabled the first use of Heracleides of Tarentum's siege engine, the sambuca
work: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes

front: Event: Archimedes was hired to build a giant claw to shake enemy ships and a heat ray of parabolic mirrors to defend the city
work: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes

front: Event: when the city fell in 212 BCE, Archimedes was killed by a Roman soldier despite his last words, "do not disturb my circles"
work: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes

front: Ruler: the last tyrant of Syracuse, who allegedly tried to defend the city using a giant ship-burning mirror
work: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes
